sections.text: For my first exploration into the UK fashion scene, I decided to take a short trip to Shoreditch before my Wednesday class. My first comparative observation coming from the States was quite a clear distinction; fashion in London does not force itself upon you in the way I am used to.

sections.heading: Uniqlo
sections.text: My favorite brand was Uniqlo, a store I often visit for its low prices and high-quality materials. Walking into the store, I could hardly see the bold signs I expected; instead, I walked upon a row of columns, within which Uniqlo sat. I was greeted by a guard and the whole process seemed to be of my own creation. Similar to the stores in the US, 90% of the clothes were folded neatly on shelves with an image of a flowing shirt or pants nearby. I noticed many mannequins that displayed a large emphasis on layering, alongside an automated consumer checkout station. When I went to try some pieces on, the process was exactly what I anticipated. I brought two shirts to try on, and was handed a number 2, being told to go to fitting room six. The whole shopping experience felt extraordinarily transactional, many sizes were out of stock, and I was directed to go to the website to find exactly what I was looking for. In many ways, the experience felt exactly like it does in the States, with perhaps a slight difference in how the mannequins were dressed for London weather. I purchased a casual shirt that is as basic as can be, and went to the second store on my list.

images.alt: Uniqlo store entrance
Uniqlo clothing display
Uniqlo mannequins
Uniqlo interior

sections.heading: Goodhood
sections.text: As I walked on the road towards Goodhood, I instantly felt a more curated atmosphere. The windowfronts were intensely artistic, calling into question what the store was actually selling. A very pleasant aroma was wafting through the front entrance, and the clothes felt like individual pieces, all hanging and with lots of separation between each garment. Many pairs of unique-looking shoes lined the walls alongside some interactive art pieces, such as a music booth. The store seemed curated towards a more edgy crowd that values their clothes and has a somewhat high willingness to pay. I grabbed a hat that I liked and identified a short-sleeve button-up. While I was looking at the shirt, the impeccable customer service displayed itself. They offered to hold the hat I was carrying at the register and to let me try on the shirt in whatever size I would like. Goodhood clearly makes a point. What they sell is cool, and they let the customer know that by making their store a cool place to be. Unlike Uniqlo, Goodhood cultivated a shopping experience, one where the customer will remember the purchasing experience every time they wear their piece.

images.alt: Goodhood storefront
Goodhood interior
Goodhood display
Goodhood shoes
Goodhood clothing
Goodhood details

sections.heading: Brick Lane Vintage Market
sections.text: The third and perhaps most unique experience I had was at the Brick Lane Vintage Market. Tucked away on a quiet street, I found a purple-lit staircase leading into an expansive room with many small second-hand clothing shops. Music was blasting from various vendors and the owners were ready to engage with you about their collections amidst the chaotic frenzy. I saw some very fashionable outfits donned by the shopkeepers, fashion was clearly their passion and by extension their livelihood. As I walked around the various stores, I felt a strong sense of discovery. Each owner's passions were displayed clearly, some owners loved bags, some loved overcoats. The specialization was incredible, and each store had something unique to offer. Brick Lane felt fundamentally different than the other two stores, here, fashion was a lifestyle, and they invited you into their worlds, not the other way around. If you aren't interested in what they are selling, they aren't interested in you, and if you find interest, the bond created with the shopowner was instantaneous. Each of them instantly invited me to try on several items, and price negotiation was on the table, especially if you wanted to buy a couple of items. I have had similar experiences to this in the US, except Brick Lane felt more personal on both sides. I witnessed shopkeepers drilling into their walls as they added more hooks, and they readily showed me around their stock to help me find items they thought would look good on me. The permanence of the market made it feel real and passionate, far less transactory than the experiences I have had in the States at similar establishments.

images.alt: Brick Lane Vintage Market entrance
Brick Lane vendors
Brick Lane vintage clothing

sections.text: This walk was the perfect starting point for my fashion exploration in London. It gave me a perception of the passionate scene, alongside the high-end experiences London has to offer. I reaffirmed my desire to engage with the fashion community here in London, and it helped me realize that I want to find items that make me feel more like me.

featuredImage.alt: White draped goddess dress with gold sun brooch by Di Petsa

sections.text: My first impression of the Barbican was the intense brutalist architecture. I tend to love this style. I watched the brutalists when it came out in theaters, and it sparked a deep respect for its origins as an architectural style. I think that brutalist architecture perfectly exemplifies what the dirty looks exhibit. A complete disregard for shame, and an admiration for beauty in the natural messiness of the world.

images.alt: Barbican brutalist architecture exterior
Barbican concrete walkway and pillars

sections.heading: Dimitra Petsa
sections.text: When prompted to find a specific designer I resonated with, I quickly found myself in the Leaky Bodies section. What drew me in was the video that was playing in efforts to curate the room titled “Dirty Girls.” Interestingly, I had come across this video previously. It was posted 12 years ago on YouTube, and when I watched it for the first time, I remember being struck by the bold, unbothered nature of the video’s subjects. The designer in this section of the exhibit that I resonated with the most, and whom I felt connected to the “Dirty Girls” video the most, was Dimitra Petsa. Famous for her “Wetlook” designs, Di Petsa embodies attitudes such as confidence and self-worth. Her works vehemently reject shame regarding the female body, and I really enjoyed the works she had on display.

images.alt: Exhibit mannequins displaying Di Petsa designs
White draped gown on display

sections.text: The pieces on display were a wetlook design, bloodied underwear, and pee-soaked pants. I found them incredibly compelling, and they stopped me in my tracks, not only because of the sharp, bold nature of the display, but also because of the connection they had with the video “Dirty Girls.”

images.alt: White draped goddess dress with gold sun brooch
Sheer top with pee-stain trousers
Blue corset with bloodied underwear

sections.text: The “Dirty Girls” express their creativity and individuality through their dirty garments, and as a result, they feel more confident because they are living the way they want to live. I observed that Di Petsa does the same with her garments. Her works are meant to embolden the wearer while allowing an easy disregard for anything that does not agree with their boldness.

images.alt: Fashion sketch of Di Petsa designs
Sketching alongside the exhibit mannequins

sections.text: Overall, I really enjoyed the Barbican. It was bold, and the designs did not invite judgment, only observation. I think that the type of fashion shown in the Dirty Looks exhibit is perfect for someone looking to learn more about fashion. As someone who knows little about high fashion, I took away many valuable insights regarding the different styles and creativity that go into the looks presented.

featuredImage.alt: Harrods department store exterior at night

sections.text: When walking into Herrods, I was instantly washed over with a slight sense of familiarity. I was reminded of similar luxury shopping experiences in New York, such as Saks, Bloomingdale's, and Bergdorf's. However, as I walked through the various floors, I was overwhelmed by the sheer scale of Harrods. I have never stepped foot in a Luxury shopping space that can compete with the scale that Harrods boasts. Not only does Herrods showcase an abundance of designer goods, but it does so in an opulent setting, the building's trim perfectly exemplifies what lies within. Millions worth of merchandise and countless hours of designers' time.

images.alt: Harrods perfume hall with chandeliers
Harrods floor directory
Harrods Egyptian escalators
Luxury fabric display

sections.text: It was the single most overwhelming and amazing walk I have had in quite some time. Hundreds of nicely dressed shopping assistants looked me up and down, assessing if I was a potential customer. Many asked if I was alright or if I was looking for anything in particular. It was an experience to note, and so were the customers. Many of the customers were dressed to the nines and carried multiple bags from various designer stores.

images.alt: Burberry entrance
Issey Miyake store
McQueen bag display

sections.text: The shopping experience felt intimidating yet intriguing to me; to many, however, it seemed like a performance. They were letting the world know they shopped luxuriously, and they did not mind being seen. This style of shopping is the most substantial similarity between Herrods and the stores in New York that are similar to it. People gain a certain status or impression of such when they make large purchases and carry around large bags. Shopping at Harrods is much more of a performance than a practical shopping experience.

images.alt: Hermès scarf display
Tom Ford suit display
Luxury suits display
Designer fragrance collection

sections.text: Overall, Herrods taught me that sometimes, it is the experience in itself that people crave. To know that something is desired further compels their purchasing decision. Herrods caters to a particular crowd, and that crowd wants to feel like they are getting the best of the best.

images.alt: Mannequin in illuminated display
Fashion mannequin display

featuredImage.alt: Interacting with a costumed performer at Venice Carnival

sections.text: During a recent trip, I visited Italy, an energetic hub for fashion, and I would love to share my experience. My first stop was Venice for the Carnival, a two-week festival celebrating 12th-century Italian traditions. During the Carnival, people dress in elaborate masks and costumes and parade through the city. There are public plays in the squares, and costume competitions to reward the most elaborate costumes. Many people clearly spent countless hours designing their costumes. They stood as symbols of the tradition that has run for hundreds of years.

images.alt: Interacting with a costumed performer at Venice Carnival

sections.text: Of course, when visiting, I had to participate; however, I did not want to buy a cheap plastic mask that would soon be thrown away. Due to my desire to purchase something that would last, I took the time to find a high-quality, handcrafted mask. I went to a highly rated shop, and there were hundreds of masks lined up, each slightly unique. The first mask I picked up stuck with me as I tried on others; it was destined to be. I purchased the mask I was initially drawn to and wore it the rest of the day. Purchasing a handcrafted mask was important to the Carnival’s custom, as it represents the work people put into their craft to support their traditions. With many shops selling cheap plastic masks, I was glad that I purchased one that I can keep for years to come.

images.alt: Handcrafted mask shop interior in Venice
Masks and leather goods hanging from ceiling

sections.text: The Carnival as a whole was almost like a display; everyone was wearing either full costumes or small masks, and the costumes were being flaunted for everyone to see. People in full costumes carried themselves as if they were on the runway; they allowed photos and were definitely hoping to get recognition by winning the costume contest. The way people in elaborate costumes carried themselves, in a way, echoed the performative wear of the people shopping at Harrods. They spent a lot of time and money on their costumes, and they wanted to be seen and recognized for their efforts.

I had two very alternate experiences in Milan. I experienced top-of-the-line luxury at an extremely famous mall at the pinnacle of global high-end fashion. The Quadrilatero della Moda was very similar to Harrods; however, there were definitely more people simply window shopping than in Harrods. Due to its open, very public nature, the Quadrilatero della Moda was an experience open to all, but the stores were more selective. Each with its own doorman, you really needed to be shopping for the goods inside to enter. There were long lines outside the shops, though only a couple of people were inside; it was very public, yet it still felt exclusive.

images.alt: Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II interior
Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II with Prada storefront
Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II luxury shops

sections.text: The other side of Milan I witnessed was the charity shop side. Many of the shops I went into were displaying used high-end goods, and I found that interesting. There was a whole ecosystem of charity shops, ranging in price. I really enjoyed my experience; the amount of leather goods was extreme, and overall, the items in the charity shops were very fashionable. It was clear that Milan residents donated/sold these goods to shops; the items offered a stylish yet attainable shopping experience. Every shop owner was accepting, and they welcomed me to come in and look at the goods, regardless of the price. I ended up purchasing some jeans and a Ralph Lauren jacket at a charity shop, which is relevant because Ralph Lauren is my group brand. I found that although it is an American brand, the price was quite low compared to what I would have found in the States.

images.alt: Browsing leather goods at a Milan charity shop
Charity shop clothing racks
Colorful clothing on display at charity shop
Milan charity shop interior

sections.text: Compared to the London fashion scene, I learned that the Italian scene is much more curated and selective. There was a strong emphasis on leather goods rather than on many British brands. The international designers were still present in Italy; however, the number of handmade Italian leather goods shops far surpassed what I would expect to see in London, and they clearly have found their profitable niche.

featuredImage.alt: Rag & Bone window display in Amsterdam

sections.text: I traveled to Amsterdam and, while there, took careful note of the various styles of people around me and the shopping atmosphere. What I initially noticed was the strong bike culture. Nearly all the locals biked from point A to B; as a result, many of the outfits aligned with this practice. Most of the pants worn were slim/skinny, and few people wore long overcoats. Many of the women wore pants rather than flowy skirts/dresses; the fashion culture clearly prioritized wearability and movement over curated, “large” looks.

images.alt: Rows of bicycles lining an Amsterdam street at night
Uniqlo store entrance in Amsterdam
Uniqlo window display with casual denim looks

sections.text: The local fashion scene seemed dressed down compared to the culture in London, such as the performative fashion at Harrods. With that said, quality should not always align with this performative nature; rather, the opposite is often true. I imagine Dutch culture prioritizes high-quality, long-lasting clothing items that can withstand the test of time. While their clothes are significantly less for show, they are still designed to last and often look high-quality.

sections.heading: Rag & Bone
sections.text: The store I interacted with most was a Rag & Bone store. Rag & Bone, a New York brand, had quite an extensive store, with bold, Amsterdam-themed window displays. The mannequins were understated, yet fashionable. The clothes inside emphasise quality and function first, fashion second. Rag and Bone is famous for its alternative-material jeans; these were a major selling point at their Amsterdam location. They were clearly marketing their comfort and function-first garments, showcasing how a customer can still look fashionable without sacrificing movement and comfort.

images.alt: Rag & Bone window display with mannequins in understated looks
Rag & Bone store interior with New York branding
Rag & Bone alternative-material jeans on display
Rag & Bone jeans collection and sneakers display

sections.text: Exploring Amsterdam’s fashion scene allowed me to appreciate the function-first clothing industry. I am not a large fan of visible labels, and I love it when clothes last even in the most extreme environments. I will take inspiration from the culture in Amsterdam to ensure that I don’t sacrifice too much bodily function/comfort for fashion.

featuredImage.alt: John wearing a faded grey leather jacket found at the High Holborn thrift store

sections.text: For a while now, I have had a singular, brown leather jacket. This week, it was finally time for me to expand my wardrobe and invest in another leather piece. I had some free time and, while doing research, identified a thrift store/charity shop that caught my interest. I wanted to find a jacket that fit what I was looking for, and importantly, did not smell musty. This may come as a shock, or maybe not, but often, cluttered charity shop finds look amazing, but boast a musty smell that is hard to look past. Located in High Holborn, I hoped my selected store would not only have a large selection, but the quality would be slightly better, and the garments would be must free. While this may come alongside slightly higher prices, it was a price I was willing to pay.

images.alt: Storefront entrance of the High Holborn thrift store with yellow mural wall
Inside the thrift store showing curated layout with Fresh Delivery sign and neatly organized clothing

sections.text: Walking up to the shop, I began to see many young people clearly into the thrifting culture. The outfits I was seeing within a two-block radius became vastly more experimental, a very good sign and something I mentally took note of. I walked into the store, and it was quite sizable, sectioned off by clothing items, and color-coded. This shop was a curated thrift store, offering a more high-end shopping experience than standard charity shops. There were designer racks, brand-specific racks, and clothing genre racks. The pricing was quite modest; however, it was likely higher than most comparable shops in London. Interestingly, however, the prices were lower than comparable stores in New York, such as 2nd Street.

images.alt: Color-coded clothing racks organized in a rainbow spectrum
Arc'teryx and Carhartt jackets on the designer rack
Boots neatly organized by color on built-in shelves

sections.text: I eventually made my way to the leather jacket section. I was initially looking for a black leather jacket; however, when I stumbled across a faded grey leather jacket, I fell in love at first sight. I tried it on, and loved the way it fit. It was a very casual-fitting jacket, starkly contrasting with the very formal brown jacket I have. I loved the contrasting styles. I was realistically looking for a jacket I could wear in situations where the brown one didn't feel appropriate, and this was the perfect match. The second I tried it on, I didn't let go of it as I shopped for a sweater for my friend. The fact that I was able to find a perfect match showcases the large selection of desirable pieces the High Holborn thrifting scene offers.

images.alt: Rack of leather jackets in the thrift store
John wearing the faded grey leather jacket in a mirror selfie at the store

sections.text: I loved the High Holborn thrift store; it was a curated experience, and it felt easy to find good items. The whole experience felt like something I had experienced before, both in my hometown of Portland, Oregon, a hub for both typical and curated charity shops, and New York, where the expensive and curated charity shops dominate.

featuredImage.alt: The ornate stone exterior of the Uniqlo store in Marseille

sections.text: Arriving in Marseille, clothing and fashion were not really on my mind. I was focused on the desire to go to the beach, get tan, and eat good food. Unfortunately, the weather was disappointing, with temperatures hardly above 60 degrees Fahrenheit. Additionally, both of my companions were overwhelmed and extremely busy with schoolwork and interview prep. As a result of these factors, I was left alone for two consecutive days to entertain myself in the cold French riverara. I took to exploring the streets.
As I walked aimlessly, I came across a street that made the crowds at Grand Central Terminal seem small. At the end of the walkway was a palace, and on the sides were countless clothing shops.

images.alt: Bustling Marseille street packed with crowds, ornate buildings and a palace visible at the far end

sections.text: Something I was not expecting was the amount of fast fashion and budget stores present. Not all of them were large names or even official stores; many stores boasted cheap items on outdoor racks. The closest experiences I have felt to the shopping experience were the streets near Fordham in the Bronx, where outdoor sellers sell alongside established storefronts. A similar comparison would be clothing booths at Saturday markets or along the street that sell I Heart NY shirts and cheap knockoff Yankees hats.

images.alt: Kilo Shop storefront with clothing piled on outdoor racks and a Prix au Poids sign
Outdoor clothing rack on a Marseille street with budget items for sale
Inside a cluttered budget clothing store packed with shirts, shoes, and accessories
Vintage shop interior with jackets on a rack alongside antique frames and décor

sections.text: One store on this street, however, which has been a recurring theme among my blog posts, stopped me in my tracks. It was the most elegant Uniqlo that I have ever seen. Beautiful stonework on the outside, and ornate railings in the interior, this Uniqlo was something special. Clearly, they had repurposed some old piece of architecture and turned it into the most classy Uniqlo I have ever seen. The clothing arrangement was largely consistent with other Uniqlo stores around the world, with many items folded and heavily layered mannequins. Only the most expensive items were hung, and many items were so small that they were in boxes and could not be tried on. I was amazed by this Uniqlo, especially considering that its surroundings were not particularly nice to look at, nor expensive. Uniqlo in Marseille is truly a classy one; they continued with their discreet signage, but the store opened up into a very ornate shopping experience.

images.alt: Uniqlo exterior in Marseille housed in a grand stone building with ornate carvings
Inside the Marseille Uniqlo showing ornate railings, high ceilings, and elegant architectural details
Uniqlo interior with layered mannequins and folded items on shelves

featuredImage.alt: Uniqlo mannequin with layered clothing beneath an ornate chandelier in Barcelona

sections.text: Upon arrival in Barcelona, my first impression of the fashion scene was that it prioritized comfort, especially during the hot weather. While many people did not wear jackets, when they did, I saw lots of leather and soccer (football) related themes. The outer garments ranged from something easy to carry in hand to something thin, meant to break wind. I did not sense an overly formal fashion scene; most people preferred a casual style, wearing t-shirts, blue jeans, and sweaters. The two main focal points during my trip were the charity/budget shops, Uniqlo, and Zara as an honorable mention.

images.alt: Leather jacket shop window display in Barcelona
Barcelona football memorabilia store with FC Barcelona shirts, hats, and souvenirs

sections.text: Barcelona had the most interesting charity shop networks that I have seen on my travels thus far. By far, Barcelona had the lowest prices, while not sacrificing brand names/reputable pieces. The only comparable location I have been to that rivals Barcelona is Berlin; however, while they are similar, they are also vastly different. Berlin boasted an amazing network of thrift/charity shops; however, the prices were high, and while the pieces were very cool and dark, Barcelona's network showcased a wide array of different options for incredibly low prices. We explored one store that sold most of its items for as low as 1 euro. This is incredibly cheap, and the clothes were surprisingly of very good quality. While there was little curation in Barcelona, I was able to find brands that I knew. While they were not necessarily high-end brands, the shopping experience was still very fun. I ended up purchasing a sweater that is a brand I recognize for 1 euro, and it looks very nice. While Barcelona's thrift culture was vastly less curated than Berlin's and London's, it made up for this with its incredibly low prices and amazing variety of items.

images.alt: Charity shop with All Clothes 1 euro signs and racks of jackets
Inside the 1 euro charity store with packed clothing racks
Outdoor charity shop with clothing and accessories displayed on the street

sections.text: In addition to charity shops, I also went to a large shopping district in Barcelona where there were massive retail stores. I saw the world's largest Zara, and the building was genuinely huge. I visited lots of soccer (football) memorabilia stores, and of course, I had to see Uniqlo in Barcelona. Similar to the store in Mariselle, Barcelona's Uniqlo was within a massive historical building. It took up 4 floors and was very ornate inside. The arrangement of the clothing was very similar to other stores internationally; however, the surroundings were much nicer, similar to the store in Mariselle. Compared to London and American Uniqlo, the Barcelona Uniqlo was very fancy and created a very nice environment. There was even a chandelier, something I haven't seen at any Uniqlo before. Something that remained the same was the style of the mannequins' clothing. Many of them had 3-5 layers on, and, interestingly, many were wearing hats or sunglasses, clearly a small nod to the strong sun nearby.

images.alt: The massive Zara building exterior in Barcelona's shopping district
Dramatic store interior with colorful columns and mannequin display
FC Barcelona jerseys including Lewandowski and Lamine Yamal on display
Uniqlo mannequin with layered outfit beneath an ornate chandelier
Inside Barcelona Uniqlo showing ornate ceiling, hung items, and boxed accessories
Uniqlo interior with folded clothing displays and mannequins in a historic building

sections.text: I enjoyed the Barcelona shopping experience. It definitely was a less formal, more fast/affordable fashion experience, as I witnessed; however, there were many great deals and amazing items to be found.
